question: How do I know if my windows are square enough for inside-mount roller shades?
answer: Measure both diagonals of your window opening—from top-left to bottom-right, and from top-right to bottom-left. If those two diagonal measurements differ by more than 1/4 inch, the window is too far out of square for reliable inside-mount roller shade performance. Roller shades are rigid systems that demand geometric precision; cellular shades and shutters are more forgiving of out-of-square openings.

question: What happens if I install roller shades in an out-of-square window?
answer: The shade will track unevenly as it rolls up and down—one side will have more fabric than the other, creating tension imbalance. This causes the fabric to drift toward one side, creating light gaps on the other. Over time, the uneven stress accelerates wear on the fabric and mechanism. These aren't problems that go away with adjustments; they're physics problems caused by a mismatched geometric relationship between the shade and the opening.

question: Are there good options if my windows fail the squareness test?
answer: Yes, two excellent alternatives. Outside-mount roller shades mounted on the wall rather than inside the frame bypass the squareness issue entirely—you create your own level mounting surface. Cellular shades are the second option; their flexible fabric absorbs minor geometric imperfections that rigid roller tubes cannot accommodate. Both deliver excellent light control and can be motorized if desired.
